text: High-intensity interval training produces a large improvement in VO2max of approximately 5.5 mL/kg/min compared to no-exercise controls.
Continuous endurance training produces a large improvement in VO2max of approximately 4.9 mL/kg/min compared to no-exercise controls.
HIT elicits a possibly small but meaningful additional VO2max improvement of 1.2 mL/kg/min compared to continuous endurance training.
Individuals with lower baseline fitness show a greater VO2max response to HIT, with a moderate additional increase of 3.2 mL/kg/min compared to fitter individuals.
population: healthy adults with lower baseline fitness

text: Longer HIT intervention durations produce a moderate additional VO2max improvement of 3.0 mL/kg/min compared to shorter interventions.
Longer HIT repetition durations produce greater VO2max gains when compared head-to-head with endurance training, with a small additional improvement of 2.2 mL/kg/min.
Older adults within the 18-45 age range show a greater relative VO2max benefit from HIT over endurance training, with a small additional improvement of 1.8 mL/kg/min.
A greater work-to-rest ratio in HIT protocols produces a small additional VO2max benefit of 1.6 mL/kg/min when HIT is compared with endurance training.
